articles.title: Are you a woman who makes life easier for everyone else? Beware – you could endanger your health | Emma Beddington
articles.description: A new claim is doing the rounds online: that women who are too nice risk getting an autoimmune disease. And while aspects of this message are clearly dubious, there’s a reason it is resonating Women, a warning from Instagram : “You really need to be a bitch or you’re going to develop an autoimmune disease. It’s that simple.” Versions of this scientifically dubious statement have caught the imagination of a corner of the internet, getting algorithmically nudged my way multiple times (a TikTok to this effect has 40,000 likes; a Threads post 26,000). Sometimes, it’s set to music; sometimes, it’s the basis for earnest discussion of cortisol and inflammation. Sometimes, it’s evangelical. One woman claims that, “Being a bitch healed my autoimmune disease,” adding: “Being the ‘love and light’ spiritual girlie is probably the reason why you feel depressed and you have IBS.” A Substack evokes the need to break the “good girl contract”, talking about those for whom “setting boundaries, getting ferocious about protecting their own bodies, minds, souls ... sometimes allowed the nervous system to settle enough that the body’s natural self-healing mechanisms could kick in and heal”. As a woman with an autoimmune condition (alopecia), this resonates on a woo-woo level: my hair fell out when I was trying and failing to reconcile incompatible demands; to make everyone happy. It’s also, I recognise, deeply silly. For a start, “women” – yes, all of us – needing to do something, or be a certain way, is a wild generalisation. It’s also definitively not “that simple”, and I would hate to upset a whole community of intellectually rigorous immunologists. I imagine them rhythmically banging their heads against their keyboards, muttering about there being no peer-reviewed cohort studies interrogating the relationship between “being the love and light spiritual girlie”, or putting too many exclamation markers and conciliatory qualifiers in emails, and autoimmune disease. Continue reading...

articles.title: Compass Pathways Cheers Trump Order To Accelerate Access To Psychedlic Drug Treatements: 'Recognizes Profound Urgency Of Mental Health Crisis'
articles.description: Compass Pathways PLC (NASDAQ: CMPS ) said a White House executive order aimed at accelerating mental health treatment research could help speed access to its experimental psilocybin therapy, which the company says has shown fast and durable results in late-stage clinical trials for treatment-resistant depression. White House Order Boosts Psychedelic Drug Momentum The biotechnology company Compass Pathways welcomed the Trump administration's move, calling it an important step toward addressing the mental health crisis. Compass CEO Kabir Nath said the executive order "recognizes the profound urgency of the mental health crisis facing millions of Americans and the potential impact FDA-approved psychedelics could have." Nath said the policy "aligns regulatory urgency with patient need" and supports "accelerating access, without compromising rigorous science." He added that the company's synthetic psilocybin drug, COMP360, has shown "highly statistically significant and clinically meaningful data" in Phase 3 trials for treatment-resistant depression. The company said ... Full story available on Benzinga.com

articles.title: Healers who kill
articles.description: A DECADE and roughly 10 human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) outbreaks later, it’s clear we’ve learned little, if anything. Consider Taunsa: between November 2024 and October 2025, 331 children tested positive for HIV there. Eight months after a government crackdown on the Tehsil Headquarters Hospital, where the medical superintendent was removed and replaced, incriminating undercover footage by the BBC shows not much has changed. Syringes being reused on multiple children, injections administered through clothing, a nurse rummaging through medical waste without gloves — these malpractices have been linked to the outbreak. The video was complemented by parents’ confirmation of the reuse of syringes on their children. In more than half of these cases, a “contaminated needle” was identified as the mode of transmission. With the health system a vortex of infection, and HIV no longer confined to certain high-risk groups, former SAPM on health, Dr Zafar Mirza, has waved a red flag in a recent tweet : “We are sitting on a generalised epidemic of AIDS in Pakistan”, adding that estimates by UNAIDS point to Pakistan as the country with the fastest-growing number of AIDS cases in Asia. The all-too-familiar cycle will unfold: eyebrows will rise, outrage will flare and the finger-pointing will begin, followed by inquiries. The media will whip up a frenzy, the government will rap a few knuckles — and before long, it will all fade from our collective memory, until the next outbreak forces it back into view. This pattern has been observed across four major outbreaks: in 2019, Larkana reported over 1,000 cases, up to 90 per cent of them children; in 2023, Jacobabad and Shikarpur became hotspots; in 2024, alongside Taunsa, Mirpurkhas emerged as “another Larkana” after 150 children tested positive, while in south Punjab, Dera Ghazi Khan saw a surge in HIV cases linked to unsafe sexual practices and the reuse of syringes by unqualified practitioners. In 2025, Hyderabad, Shaheed Benazirabad, Naushahro Feroze and Karachi’s Pathan Colony also made headlines for rising HIV cases. Parents took their sick children to those they trusted would heal them — only to return with a far more serious, lifelong infection. Yet, these criminal practices have failed to provoke the outrage they deserve from the public, health practitioners and even governments. No­­bo­­dy seems to know who to hold accountable, otherwise, why would these outbreaks continue so frequently? And in the Taunsa hospital’s case, in par­ticular, it is clear that the irresponsible behaviour never stopped despite a change in leadership. Along with implementation gaps, the lack of public awareness remains a stumbling block. Patients, especially in public hospitals, are not only poor, but often poorly informed. They have little reason to suspect that a routine injection could leave them or their child with a lifelong infection. In rural and peri-urban communities, this already vulnerable population is left at the mercy of practitioners who can be insensitive, unskilled — even cruel. With little oversight or accountability, these health practitioners operate with near impunity. When senior officials dismiss the BBC ’s evidence of “repeated and serious breaches of basic infection control” as “staged”, it raises a stark question: what real hope is there for accountability or recourse? Another question that comes to mind is: why are these outbreaks happening in the first place? Is there an incentive to overuse injections and why is enforcement against syringe and needle reuse by the provincial health authorities weak, despite the various plans, policies and strategies? Four major policy responses — the National Injection Safety Policy (2004–05), the Prime Minister’s Programme for Prevention and Control of Hepatitis (launched in 2005), the National Action Plan to Address Unsafe Injection Practices (2019-2021) and the National Infection Prevention & Control Strategic Framework/Guidelines (2021–23; 2025–30) — were all born out of deadly hepatitis C and HIV outbreaks, with the explicit aim of ending unsafe injections and syringe reuse. Yet, despite the urgency that produced them, these frameworks appear to have remained largely on paper, as can be seen by the upsurge (we have around 10 million people infected with hepatitis C, the largest burden in the world) in these diseases. Injection and transfusion safety are the health system’s “nemesis”, says Karachi-based paediatric infectious disease specialist Dr Fatima Mir — a basic standard that demands strict compliance at every level, yet remains stubbornly unenforced. What makes these outbreaks especially tragic is that over 80 per cent of those diagnosed are children — an anomaly in HIV epidemiology, since children are not engaged in high-risk behaviours such as unprotected sex or injecting drug use. With most mothers testing negative, mother-to-child transmission has also been ruled out. Parents took their sick children to those they trusted would heal them — only to return with a far more serious, lifelong infection. The child will require lifelong antiretroviral therapy, but with uneven access, it may mean inconsistent treatment. With funding under pressure and the disease burden rising, Pakistani children risk being further shortchanged. But even if medicines are there and provided free of cost, the long distances to the AIDS Control Programme’s 96 ART centres pose another challenge for families already stretched thin. One may roundly condemn the reuse of single-use injection equipment, but unless safe alternatives in the form of good quality oral medication, strict use of single-lock syringes with extras available for a single person and clear SOPs on safe sterile preparation of multiple doses from single-use vials in quality-assured pharmacies are not aggressively implemented, we will see a snowball effect on HIV prevalence in children in Pakistan, through no fault of their own, Dr Mir stresses. Still, it is worth emphasising: HIV is not the only infection of concern, nor are reused syringes the sole route of transmission. Reused blades, such as those used by barbers, and unscreened, contaminated blood transfusions, for instance, in children with thalassemia, are equally dangerous. These same pathways also spread hepatitis B and C, compounding a public health crisis that is entirely preventable yet continues to repeat itself, outbreak after outbreak. The writer is an independent journalist based in Karachi. X: @zofeen28 Published in Dawn, April 19th, 2026

articles.title: ‘I was fighting two wars’: Ukraine’s soldiers confront their addiction struggles
articles.description: Troops frequently use substances to help cope with untreated PTSD and anxiety, producing a negative spiral Seven years clean, Oleksandr believed he had left addiction behind. Then, a year into fighting Russia, the Ukrainian soldier was prescribed painkillers for a shoulder injury. Under the strain of war, he relapsed and quickly began using stronger illicit opioids. “From that moment, I was fighting two wars – one inside myself and one with Russia,” he said, speaking at a rehabilitation facility in Kyiv. Oleksandr relapsed into addiction after treatment for a shoulder injury sustained during fighting. Continue reading...

articles.title: Scientists say this type of olive oil could boost brain power
articles.description: Extra virgin olive oil might help protect your brain by working through your gut. A two-year study found that people who consumed it had better cognitive performance and more diverse gut bacteria than those using refined olive oil. Researchers even identified specific microbes linked to these benefits. The findings suggest that choosing high-quality olive oil could be a simple way to support brain health as you age.

articles.title: Direct Meds GLP-1 2026: Claims Examined, Pricing Verified & What Consumers Should Confirm Before Buying
articles.description: New York City, NY, April 18, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- You've done the diets. Probably more than once. You've tracked calories, cut carbs, tried intermittent fasting, started gym memberships in January and quietly stopped going in March. And none of it has moved the needle the way you needed it to. Here's something worth understanding: for many people, that struggle isn't about willpower. It's about biology. The human body is built to resist weight loss, hunger hormones ramp up, metabolism slows down, and the brain sends relentless signals to eat more. For decades, conventional dieting tried to override this system through restriction alone. It's a hard way to fight when the odds are against you. GLP-1 medications, a class of prescription treatments that work directly on the hormonal signals that control hunger and fullness, represent one of the most significant shifts in medically supervised weight management in recent memory. In large clinical trials of FDA-approved GLP-1 active ingredients, participants lost a significant amount of weight when they also followed a diet and exercised. Results may vary based on starting weight and program adherence. Direct Meds is a telehealth platform that connects patients with licensed physicians who can evaluate whether a compounded GLP-1 medication may be appropriate for their individual needs and, if prescribed, facilitate access to it with shipping included. No insurance hoops. No clinic waiting rooms. No pharmacy trips. Is it the right path for you? That's what this review breaks down. What Is Direct Meds? Direct Meds is a U.S.-based telehealth platform that provides patients with access to licensed physicians who evaluate their suitability for compounded GLP-1 weight management medications. It is not a pharmacy and not a drug manufacturer; it is the access layer between patients seeking medically supervised weight support and the licensed medical professionals who make all prescribing decisions. The platform has served over 180,000 customers and holds LegitScript certification, a recognized standard for legitimate online healthcare platforms that verifies compliance with applicable laws and regulations. What Direct Meds offers through its program: Following a completed online medical intake, patients are connected with board-certified physicians who review their health history and determine whether a compounded GLP-1 medication is clinically appropriate. If prescribed, medications are prepared by U.S.-based compounding pharmacies and shipped directly to the patient's door. Two formats available: Direct Meds currently offers compounded GLP-1 medications in both injectable and sublingual (oral drop) formats, giving patients and their physicians flexibility based on individual preference and clinical suitability. Important distinction: Compounded medications are not FDA-approved. They are prepared by licensed compounding pharmacies to meet individual patient needs when commercially available options are not suitable for their personalized needs. The active ingredients used, semaglutide and tirzepatide, are the same active ingredients found in several FDA-approved products, but the compounded formulations themselves have not been reviewed by the FDA for safety, effectiveness, or manufacturing quality. Direct Meds operates as the coordination platform: it handles the intake process, the telehealth consultation, and the logistics. The prescribing and dispensing are handled by independent licensed physicians and separate licensed pharmacies. Does Direct Meds Really Work? To answer this clearly, it's important to separate two distinct questions: does the Direct Meds platform work as a service, and does GLP-1 medication work as a treatment for weight management? On GLP-1 medication efficacy: The active ingredients that Direct Meds' compounded medications are based on, semaglutide and tirzepatide, have been studied extensively in large clinical trials of their FDA-approved versions. In those trials, semaglutide demonstrated average weight reductions of around 15% of body weight over 68 weeks when used alongside a reduced-calorie diet and increased physical activity. Tirzepatide showed even larger average reductions in comparable studies. These are significant outcomes from serious peer-reviewed clinical research. Note - " The active ingredients used, semaglutide and tirzepatide, are the same active ingredients found in several FDA-approved products, but the compounded formulations themselves have not been reviewed by the FDA for safety, effectiveness, or manufacturing quality." On the Direct Meds platform specifically: The service has built a strong track record as an access pathway with over 180,000 customers served, a 4.8/5 rating on Trustpilot, and LegitScript certification confirming operational standards. What the platform delivers is physician-supervised access: a licensed doctor reviews your health profile, makes an independent prescribing decision, and if appropriate, facilitates access to compounded medications prepared by licensed U.S. pharmacies. Put simply: if GLP-1 medication is appropriate for your individual situation, a decision only a licensed physician can make, Direct Meds provides a streamlined, medically supervised pathway to access it. Results may vary based on starting weight, program adherence, and individual health factors. >> For more information or to learn about available treatment options, visit DirectMeds << Is DirectMeds Legitimate? This question comes up with any telehealth platform offering compounded GLP-1 medications and it's worth answering directly, because the space has attracted some illegitimate operators alongside genuine services. Direct Meds is a legitimate platform. It holds active LegitScript certification, which requires meeting ongoing standards for licensed pharmacy partnerships, prescription practices, and regulatory compliance. It has served over 180,000 customers. It maintains a 4.8/5 rating on Trustpilot across thousands of verified reviews. Medications are dispensed by 503A-designated compounding pharmacies. The warning worth heeding about the broader market: Not all providers in this space meet the same standards. The FDA has documented counterfeit compounded GLP-1 products in the U.S. containing unknown or incorrect ingredients. When seeking any compounded medication, using a LegitScript-certified platform that partners with licensed 503A pharmacies is a meaningful quality indicator. Always ensure your medication is being prescribed by a licensed physician based on a proper medical evaluation. Direct Meds is a legitimate platform by the measurable standards available. But as with any compounded medication program, it is essential to understand that compounded formulations are not FDA-approved, the regulatory environment is evolving, and only a licensed physician can determine whether this type of treatment is appropriate for your individual health situation. >> For more information or to learn about available treatment options, visit DirectMeds << How Does Direct Meds Work? The Science Behind It Understanding Direct Meds means understanding two things: how GLP-1 medications work physiologically and how the Direct Meds platform is structured to provide physician-supervised access to them. The science behind GLP-1 medications: GLP-1 stands for glucagon-like peptide-1, a hormone your gut naturally releases in response to eating. It plays several key roles in appetite and blood sugar regulation, and GLP-1 receptor agonist medications are designed to mimic and amplify these effects. Appetite suppression via the brain: GLP-1 receptors are present in the hypothalamus, the brain region responsible for hunger and satiety signals. GLP-1 receptor activation tells your brain you are full, reducing the drive to eat. Many people on GLP-1 medications describe this as food cravings simply switching off, not a matter of willpower, but a genuine physiological shift in appetite signaling. Slowed gastric emptying: GLP-1 medications slow the rate at which food leaves the stomach. This means you feel full sooner and stay full longer after eating smaller amounts. The practical effect is a natural reduction in caloric intake without the misery of constant hunger. Blood sugar regulation: GLP-1 medications stimulate insulin release in response to food intake and suppress glucagon, the hormone that raises blood sugar. This mechanism is particularly relevant for people with blood sugar management challenges. Tirzepatide's dual mechanism: Direct Meds offers compounded formulations based on two active ingredients. Semaglutide activates GLP-1 receptors. Tirzepatide activates both GLP-1 and GIP (glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide) receptors, a dual-action approach that has been associated with larger average weight reductions in clinical trials of the FDA-approved versions. In a 2025 head-to-head clinical trial published in the New England Journal of Medicine, tirzepatide produced greater average body weight reduction over 72 weeks compared to semaglutide. These results are from studies of FDA-approved versions; compounded formulations are not FDA-approved and have not been independently reviewed for efficacy. How the Direct Meds platform works: Step 1 — Online medical intake: You complete a comprehensive health questionnaire covering your medical history, current medications, health goals, and relevant risk factors. This is the information the physician uses to evaluate your suitability. Step 2 — Physician evaluation: A licensed physician independently reviews your intake information. Telehealth consultations are included. The physician makes all prescribing decisions based on your individual clinical profile, no prescription is issued automatically. Step 3 — Pharmacy fulfillment and delivery: If prescribed, your compounded GLP-1 medication is prepared by a U.S.-based 503A compounding pharmacy and shipped directly to your door, typically within 1-2 days. Nursing team support is available throughout your treatment. >> For more information or to learn about available treatment options, visit DirectMeds << Powerful Features of Direct Meds: What Makes It Unique? The compounded GLP-1 telehealth space has grown considerably. Here is what specifically distinguishes the Direct Meds platform. 1. LegitScript Certified: LegitScript certification is not automatic; it requires meeting ongoing operational standards around pharmacy partnerships, prescription requirements, and regulatory compliance. Direct Meds maintains active LegitScript certification, which provides a level of third-party verification that not all providers in this space can claim. 2. Both Injectable and Oral (Sublingual) Formats: Many GLP-1 telehealth providers offer only injectable formats. Direct Meds provides both compounded injectable vials and sublingual oral drops thereby giving physicians flexibility to prescribe the format best suited to individual patient needs and preferences. 3. Dedicated Nursing Team Support: Direct Meds is one of the only providers with a dedicated nursing team that supports patients throughout their treatment. This means ongoing access to qualified clinical support for questions about dosing, side effects, titration, and progress not just a prescription and a product. 4. Telehealth Consultation Included: The physician consultation is built into the program. Board-certified physicians review health profiles and make independent prescribing decisions. Telehealth visits are included, not billed separately as an add-on. 5. 1-2 Day Shipping, No Hidden Fees: If prescribed, medications ship from licensed U.S. compounding pharmacies with shipping included. The platform is transparent about pricing; same price every dose, no membership fees, cancel anytime. This is a meaningful differentiator in a space where hidden costs and recurring billing surprises are common complaints. 6. 180,000+ Patients Served: Scale matters in healthcare because it reflects operational experience managing real patient outcomes at volume. Direct Meds' track record of over 180,000 customers served provides a meaningful signal about platform reliability and operational maturity. 7. Optional B-12 Supplementation: Vitamin B-12 supplementation is included as an optional add-on at no additional charge. B-12 supports energy metabolism and neurological function, two areas commonly relevant to weight management program participants. Direct Meds Medication Options — What's Available? Direct Meds offers compounded medications prepared by licensed 503A compounding pharmacies. Here is what the program currently makes available, subject to physician prescribing decisions. Important: Compounded medications are not FDA-approved. They are prepared by licensed compounding pharmacies to meet individual patient needs when commercially available options are not suitable for their personalized needs. Efficacy data referenced below are drawn from studies of FDA-approved versions of these active ingredients, not from the compounded formulations. Medication Format Options Mechanism Compounded Semaglutide Injectable vial or sublingual drops GLP-1 receptor agonist — supports appetite regulation and gastric emptying Compounded Tirzepatide Injectable vial or sublingual drops GLP-1 and GIP dual receptor agonist — supports appetite regulation via two hormonal pathways Optional: Vitamin B-12 Included as add-on Supports energy metabolism — included at no additional charge Compounded Semaglutide — Semaglutide is the active ingredient in several FDA-approved GLP-1 products for diabetes and weight management. As a GLP-1 receptor agonist, it works by activating GLP-1 receptors in the brain and gut, suppressing appetite, slowing gastric emptying, and supporting blood sugar regulation. Direct Meds offers compounded semaglutide in both injectable and sublingual oral drop formats. The compounded version is prepared by a licensed U.S. 503A pharmacy and is not FDA-approved as a finished product. Compounded Tirzepatide — Tirzepatide is the active ingredient in several FDA-approved products for diabetes and weight management. Unlike semaglutide, which acts solely on GLP-1 receptors, tirzepatide also activates GIP receptors, a dual mechanism that clinical trials of FDA-approved tirzepatide have associated with greater average weight reductions. These results are from studies of FDA-approved versions; compounded tirzepatide is not FDA-approved and has not been independently reviewed for these outcomes. Results may vary. Injectable vs. Sublingual: The injectable format uses a standard subcutaneous injection, typically once weekly. The sublingual oral drops dissolve under the tongue. Your physician determines which format is most appropriate based on your individual clinical situation. The sublingual format is particularly relevant for patients who prefer to avoid injections entirely. >> For more information or to learn about available treatment options, visit DirectMeds << How to Access Direct Meds in 3 Simple Steps The entire process, from initial intake to receiving medication, is designed to be completed entirely online, without clinic visits, insurance submissions, or pharmacy trips. Step 1: Complete the Online Medical Intake Visit the official Direct Meds website and complete the confidential online health questionnaire. It covers your medical history, current medications, weight history, health goals, and relevant risk factors. This typically takes a few minutes and is the foundation of your physician's evaluation. Step 2: Physician Reviews and Prescribes (if appropriate) A board-certified physician independently reviews your intake information. Telehealth consultations are included to allow you to discuss your situation, ask questions about treatment options, and understand the risks and potential benefits. If the physician determines that a compounded GLP-1 medication is appropriate for your individual needs, a prescription is issued. Not every patient will qualify. Step 3: Medication Ships Directly to You If prescribed, your compounded medication is prepared by a licensed U.S. compounding pharmacy and shipped directly to your door with shipping included. Direct Meds states delivery typically within 1-2 days of order processing. Ongoing nursing team support is available throughout your treatment to address any questions about dosing, side effects, or progress. Who Can Access Direct Meds? Direct Meds is a physician-gated program meaning the physician's independent medical evaluation determines who is appropriate for treatment. That said, the following profiles are most commonly associated with suitable candidacy for compounded GLP-1 treatment based on general clinical guidance. Adults managing overweight or obesity where lifestyle interventions alone have not been sufficient. GLP-1 medications are specifically indicated in the clinical context of obesity or overweight with weight-related health conditions. If you have a BMI of 30 or above, or 27 or above with a related health condition, you may be a candidate for evaluation. Adults who have found hunger and cravings to be the primary obstacle to sustained weight loss. GLP-1 medications work on the neurological signals that drive hunger. For people who have found that diet plans fail not from lack of effort but from the relentless biological pull to eat more, this mechanism addresses the root issue not just the symptom. Adults seeking a convenient, private, fully remote program. The entire Direct Meds process happens from home. No clinic visits, no waiting rooms, ... Full story available on Benzinga.com
